subject: Urology
title: Varicocele: Why It's Almost Always Left
hook: A bag of worms that empties when he lies down, and almost always on the left. Here is why.

points.heading: The classic vignette
points.icon: a scrotum with a bag of worms
bullets: Dilated pampiniform plexus veins, a bag of worms.
Empties lying down, reappears on standing.
Non-emptying or right-sided: suspect retroperitoneal mass.

points.heading: Why the left side dominates
bullets: Left testicular vein is longer, drains the renal vein.
Nutcracker effect: left renal vein squeezed by aorta and SMA.

points.heading: How it is diagnosed
points.icon: an ultrasound probe with a vein
bullets: Ultrasound: vein diameter over about 3 mm.
Diameter alone is not enough, you need reflux.
Confirm with colour Doppler standing or on Valsalva.

points.heading: Fertility and management
bullets: Raised scrotal heat impairs spermatogenesis, causing oligospermia.
15 to 20% of men, around 40% if infertile.
Repair for atrophy, abnormal semen or symptoms; else reassure.

captionLines: A bag of worms that empties when he lies down, almost always on the left.
Left testicular vein is longer, enters the renal vein at a right angle, and gets nutcracked between aorta and SMA.
Spot the red flag: an isolated right-sided or non-emptying varicocele needs investigation for a retroperitoneal cause.
Recall Engine turns any lecture into SBAs, MCQs, flashcards and high-yield notes, so the left-side logic and reflux criteria stick before your exam.
